analysis:
- Stronger heuristics converge on the same structural recipe: pareto-filter → cheap proxy ranking (box-volume or MC) → shortlist → exact marginal HV on shortlist → bounded local swaps. This balances quality (exact HV where it matters) and CPU cost.
- Winning ideas: (a) reduce candidate set early, (b) use inexpensive proxies or MC to rank/shortlist, (c) lazy/CELF-style or timestamped gains to avoid repeated exact evaluations, (d) cap and focus local search on high-promise swaps.
- Common inefficiencies in weaker variants: repeated full hypervolume recomputations (often recreating pygmo objects per call), heavy Python-level nested loops for dominance and HV, array copies and conversions, printing/exceptions inside hot loops, and naively exploring the full n×k search leading to O(nk) or worse HV calls.
- Good tradeoffs observed: Monte‑Carlo or box‑proxy reduces HV calls at small quality loss; CELF/lazy greedy yields near-greedy quality with far fewer exact evaluations; bounded, proxy‑filtered swaps recover missed gains cheaply.
- Practical micro‑optimizations: vectorize dominance/filtering, cache singleton HVs and previously computed HVs (use tuple keys or incremental structures), reuse a single hypervolume object when possible, batch HV computations for shortlist candidates, and set deterministic random seeds for stable evaluation.
- Failure modes to guard: invalid/ill‑scaled reference points, returning wrong cardinality, and uncontrolled budgets that blow up runtime on large n or high D.
experience: Combine pareto filtering + a CELF-style lazy greedy seeded by box‑volume (or MC) proxies, cache evaluated gains with timestamps, limit shortlist size to O(k log n), and restrict swap candidates by proxy. Batch/hot-path vectorization and reusing a single hypervolume object cut overhead. Cap local‑search iterations tightly. These steps give near-greedy HV with predictable, low runtime.
current_reflection: Combine pareto filtering + a CELF-style lazy greedy seeded by box‑volume (or MC) proxies, cache evaluated gains with timestamps, limit shortlist size to O(k log n), and restrict swap candidates by proxy. Batch/hot-path vectorization and reusing a single hypervolume object cut overhead. Cap local‑search iterations tightly. These steps give near-greedy HV with predictable, low runtime.
good_reflections: Prefer a hybrid constructive: filter dominated points, approximate contributions with vectorized Monte‑Carlo or fast tensor proxies to shortlist candidates, then apply exact HV only on the shortlist with cached/incremental updates and a small swap-based local search. Vectorize dominance precomputation, avoid rebuilding objects in loops, and keep all heavy work batched.

Next generation heuristics should: (1) pareto‑filter then shortlist by cheap proxy; (2) compute exact marginal HVC only on that shortlist using cached/incremental HV data structures; (3) vectorize or Monte‑Carlo only when needed; (4) cap HV evaluations and local search adaptively; and (5) reuse pygmo objects and add deterministic seeding and parallel shortlist evaluation.
bad_reflections: Prefer a hybrid: fast nondominated filtering + inexpensive proxy ranking to build a small shortlist, then incremental/cached exact HV evaluations for greedy picks, with Monte‑Carlo or dominance masks as fallback. Limit exact HV calls and budget local swaps; vectorize dominance checks and reuse computations.
comprehensive_reflection:
- Keywords: Pareto filter, proxy shortlist (box‑volume/MC), CELF lazy greedy, cached marginals, incremental hypervolume, vectorization, bounded local swaps.
- Advice: Pareto‑filter then shortlist by cheap proxy (target ~O(k log n)); run a CELF‑style lazy greedy using cached/incremental HV gains evaluated only on the shortlist; batch/vectorize proxy and HV computations; reuse a single hypervolume object and cap local‑search iterations.
- Avoid: Rebuilding HV objects in loops, computing exact HV on all points, unbounded swap searches, stale or unversioned caches.
- Explanation: These steps minimize exact HV calls, keep work batched and incremental, and produce near‑greedy hypervolume with predictable, low runtime.
